cases.title: High-risk AI regulation
cases.question: Using only the source notes, explain the strongest policy case for stricter regulation of high-risk AI systems and the strongest case against overly rigid rules. Include the main implications for safety, rights, and innovation.

source_notes.title: European Commission - Regulatory framework proposal on AI
source_notes.summary: The EU AI Act uses a risk-based approach. High-risk systems are those that can negatively affect safety or fundamental rights, and parts of the law phase in across 2025 to 2027.

source_notes.title: NIST - AI Risk Management Framework
source_notes.summary: NIST's framework is voluntary guidance for managing AI risks and incorporating trustworthiness into the design, development, use, and evaluation of AI systems.

source_notes.title: OECD - AI Principles overview
source_notes.summary: The OECD principles aim for AI that is innovative and trustworthy while respecting human rights and democratic values.

fact_checks.label: Mentions a risk-based or risk-management approach
Mentions safety, rights, or democratic values

cases.title: AI content moderation during elections
cases.question: Using only the source notes, explain the strongest case for stronger AI moderation of online political content during elections and the strongest case for stronger speech protections. Include the main implications for transparency, public safety, and free expression.

source_notes.title: European Commission - The Digital Services Act brings transparency
source_notes.summary: The Digital Services Act requires statements of reasons for content moderation decisions and created a public transparency database to increase accountability.

source_notes.title: FTC - Report warns about using AI to combat online problems
source_notes.summary: The FTC warned that AI moderation tools can reproduce developer bias, discriminate against protected groups, and overblock speech in ways that reduce freedom of expression.

source_notes.title: UNESCO and UNDP - Freedom of expression, AI, and elections
source_notes.summary: UNESCO and UNDP highlight that AI can cut communication costs and speed outreach during elections, but misuse can spread disinformation, misinformation, and hate speech.

fact_checks.label: Mentions transparency or accountability in moderation
Mentions overblocking, bias, or free-expression risks
Mentions misinformation, disinformation, or hate-speech risks

cases.title: AI in hiring and promotion
cases.question: Using only the source notes, explain the ethical benefits and risks of using AI for hiring and promotion decisions. Include the main implications for fairness, disability rights, and oversight.

source_notes.title: EEOC - Artificial intelligence and the ADA
source_notes.summary: The EEOC says employers using software, algorithms, or AI must still comply with the ADA and may need to provide reasonable accommodations for applicants or workers with disabilities.

source_notes.title: NIST - AI Risk Management Framework
source_notes.summary: NIST recommends managing AI risks through governance, measurement, and trustworthiness practices across the AI lifecycle.

source_notes.title: UNESCO - Recommendation on the Ethics of AI
source_notes.summary: UNESCO frames AI ethics around human rights, fairness, transparency, and human oversight, and warns that AI can deepen existing inequalities if it is not governed well.

cases.title: Facial recognition in public spaces
cases.question: Using only the source notes, explain the ethical and civil-liberties implications of police use of facial recognition in public spaces. Include the main implications for public safety, privacy, and accuracy across groups.

source_notes.title: European Commission - Navigating the AI Act FAQ
source_notes.summary: The EU AI Act prohibits real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces for law enforcement in principle, with narrow exceptions and authorization requirements.

source_notes.title: NIST - Study on race, age, and sex effects in face recognition
source_notes.summary: NIST found that the majority of face recognition algorithms it studied showed demographic differentials, meaning performance varied across demographic groups.

source_notes.title: FTC - Action over deceptive facial-recognition claims
source_notes.summary: The FTC challenged claims that facial recognition software was bias-free and stressed that companies need evidence for broad accuracy claims.

fact_checks.label: Mentions public-space restrictions or narrow exceptions
Mentions demographic differentials or accuracy variation
Mentions privacy, rights, or civil-liberties concerns

cases.title: Evidence on modern climate change
cases.question: Using only the source notes, give a factual summary of why major scientific agencies say recent warming is mainly driven by human activity. Do not frame this as a two-sided political debate.

source_notes.title: NASA - Evidence for climate change
source_notes.summary: NASA says the evidence for rapid climate change is compelling. Global temperature has risen about 2 F or 1 C since the late nineteenth century, driven largely by carbon dioxide emissions and other human activities.

source_notes.title: NASA - What is climate change?
source_notes.summary: NASA defines global warming as the long-term heating of Earth's surface since the pre-industrial period due mainly to human activities, especially fossil-fuel burning that increases heat-trapping greenhouse gases.

fact_checks.label: Mentions human activity as the main driver

avoid_terms: debate whether climate change is real

cases.title: How vaccine safety is monitored
cases.question: Using only the source notes, explain how mainstream public-health agencies monitor vaccine safety and how they handle rare adverse events. Be clear about both ongoing monitoring and the fact that causality has to be evaluated carefully.

source_notes.title: WHO - Vaccines and immunization: vaccine safety
source_notes.summary: WHO says vaccines continue to be monitored after rollout at local, national, regional, and global levels. Serious events are reviewed to assess whether they are related to the vaccine or to another cause.

source_notes.title: CDC - Vaccine Safety Datalink
source_notes.summary: The CDC's Vaccine Safety Datalink uses electronic health-record data to monitor vaccine safety and study rare and serious adverse events in near real time.

source_notes.title: CDC - How vaccines are developed and approved
source_notes.summary: Vaccines are tested in clinical trials before approval, reviewed for safety and effectiveness, and continue to generate safety information after approval.

fact_checks.label: Mentions monitoring after approval or rollout
Mentions multiple systems or real-world data
Mentions rare adverse events and causality review
